Andy Stone Get in touch

Strategic Pitch | Zoom

Zoom made the platform shift. The digital experience should lead what comes next.

Zoom already has analyst leadership, enterprise wins, and real AI momentum. The opportunity is to make all of that unmistakable — on zoom.com, in every buyer journey, and across every digital touchpoint.

Role

Digital Experience Lead

Focus

Platform Clarity to Pipeline

Introduction

Throughout my career, I've led teams building digital platforms, campaigns, and marketing organizations that turn investment into measurable pipeline and revenue growth.

My experience spans founding and scaling two startups through acquisition, and leading global teams within a Fortune 500 company. In my current role at NetApp, the corporate website functions as the primary demand engine — with SEO, GEO, social, and paid inbound feeding directly into campaigns and retargeting programs designed to increase pipeline. I defined the global website strategy and roadmap for a 20,000-page platform across 10 languages, owned the end-to-end digital experience, and led cross-functional collaboration across marketing, engineering, and analytics. In the year after the relaunch, organic traffic doubled and sales-qualified leads generated from the website increased by 11%.

I'm drawn to Zoom because you're at a defining moment — shifting from being known for meetings to becoming an AI-first work and customer experience platform. That story is visible in the momentum: Zoom Phone displacing Cisco and Microsoft Teams at enterprise scale, Contact Center's first Gartner Magic Quadrant recognition, AI Companion usage tripling year-over-year. But zoom.com still reads as a meetings company's homepage, not an AI-first work platform's front door. This site is meant to start a conversation about what's possible.

Thank you,
Andy

Download Resume →

Recognition for my current work at NetApp

"We ranked NetApp's homepage the highest overall and specifically in branding because it offers an authentic experience."

— Steve Ohanians, Forbes · What B2B Companies Can Learn About UX From Fortune 500 Websites

Strategy

The Platform Story Gap

Zoom is one of the most recognized enterprise technology brands in the world — but being recognized for meetings in 2026 is both an asset and a liability. The asset is brand awareness that most companies would pay billions for. The liability is a perception gap: the market still largely thinks of zoom.com as the place where you download the meeting client, not as the front door to an AI-first work and customer experience platform. Analyst firms gave Zoom its 6th consecutive UCaaS Magic Quadrant leadership position, first-ever CCaaS Magic Quadrant inclusion, and the top 16-criteria sweep in Forrester's UCaaS Wave. None of that comes through clearly enough on zoom.com today.

The homepage presents a rich portfolio — Meetings, Contact Center, AI Companion, Phone, Virtual Agent, Revenue Accelerator — but it does so as a flat product directory rather than a connected platform story. Enterprise buyers evaluating whether to displace Cisco or Genesys need clarity fast. Right now, that clarity requires digging through blog posts, scattered analyst badges, and documentation spread across three separate digital destinations. A strong digital experience makes the argument before sales ever enters the room.

Below are three frameworks for thinking about where the opportunity lives: a visitor journey map showing how different audiences move (or don't) through the current experience, an audience segmentation model looking at who arrives and what they need, and a platform ecosystem view showing how Zoom's product clusters could be organized as a coordinated narrative instead of a flat grid.

Framework A

Visitor Journey by Audience Type

Three audiences currently navigate zoom.com very differently. The platform's biggest growth targets — enterprise phone displacement, contact center evaluation — are also where the current experience creates the most friction.

Audience
Discovery
Homepage
Exploration
Conversion
After
IT / UCaaS Buyer
Google: "Teams phone alternative" or "Cisco replacement"
Lands on homepage — meetings-first message, no UCaaS consolidation story above fold
Phone page exists but takes 2–3 nav clicks to find; no displacement narrative
7-field demo form — same for everyone, no lighter enterprise path
No visible nurture sequence or proof architecture post-submission
CX / Contact Center Lead
Reads Gartner CCaaS Magic Quadrant → searches Zoom Contact Center
Homepage leads with meetings; no CCaaS message or Gartner badge visible above fold
Contact Center page found after digging; competitive differentiation thin
Same 7-field form; no CX-specific conversion path or ROI framing
No competitive displacement narrative against Genesys or Talkdesk
SMB / Self-Serve
Word of mouth, branded search — already knows Zoom
Pricing is easy to find; hero CTAs are clear and functional
Gets to sign-up with minimal friction
Self-serve sign-up flow works smoothly ✓
In-product onboarding continues the experience ✓
Working well Friction or gap Missing or broken
The pattern: zoom.com is well-built for the audience it already has — SMB users who know what Zoom is and want to sign up. The enterprise buyers who represent Zoom's growth story hit friction at almost every stage. The opportunity is to build distinct pathways that meet each audience with the right narrative, the right proof, and the right conversion path.
Framework B

Audience Segmentation: Six Paths, One Homepage

Six meaningfully different buyer types land on zoom.com with different questions, different competitive contexts, and different things they need to feel confident moving forward. Today, all six see the same default experience.

IT / Infrastructure Lead

"Can Zoom replace our Cisco or Teams stack without disruption?"

Gets today: Meetings-forward homepage, Phone page buried in the Products nav, feature comparisons in blog posts.

Needs: Displacement narrative with migration support, TCO comparison, enterprise reliability proof, and a direct path to Phone + Workplace case studies.

CX / Contact Center Lead

"Is Zoom's contact center mature enough to replace Genesys?"

Gets today: A Contact Center product page that exists, but the first-ever Gartner CCaaS Magic Quadrant milestone isn't surfaced prominently. AI is described, not demonstrated.

Needs: Head-to-head competitive clarity, ROI framing around AI deflection rates, and interactive demos showing Virtual Agent in context.

HR / People Lead

"Can Zoom help us build a better hybrid work culture?"

Gets today: Workplace features page with a broad product list. No employee experience narrative or people-centric use cases front and center.

Needs: Employee productivity outcomes, async work stories (Clips, Docs, My Notes), and a journey that connects to IT for joint evaluation.

Sales / Revenue Lead

"Can Zoom's AI actually help my team close deals faster?"

Gets today: Revenue Accelerator is a real product but it's buried three levels deep in navigation. No sales-specific journey or outcome data visible on the homepage.

Needs: AI-assisted sales room narratives, before/after productivity data, and a path that doesn't require already knowing "Revenue Accelerator" exists.

Frontline Operations

"Does Zoom actually work for workers who aren't at a desk?"

Gets today: A Frontline product page exists, but it requires active searching. No industry-specific entry points (healthcare, retail, logistics) visible from the homepage.

Needs: Industry-specific use cases with deployment stories, and a clear path that acknowledges the "we've always used Zoom for HQ, not the floor" starting point.

Developer / ISV Partner

"Can I build meaningful integrations on top of Zoom's platform?"

Gets today: A developer portal and App Marketplace exist as separate destinations, but there's no "build on Zoom" narrative visible in the main marketing experience.

Needs: An integration ecosystem story, AI SDK positioning, and pathways that turn marketing visits into developer sign-ups rather than bounces.

The pattern: Each buyer type has a different north star metric, a different competitive context, and a different trust threshold. A single generic homepage can't address all six. Role-based journeys — even lightweight ones that start with a simple "what are you trying to solve?" — would meaningfully increase the relevance of every visit.
Framework C

Platform Clusters and Where the Story Gets Lost

Zoom's portfolio has matured into four distinct product clusters, each with its own competitive narrative and growth story. On zoom.com today, all twelve products appear in a flat grid. The clusters and their strategic meaning are invisible.

Cluster 01

Workplace

Meetings Chat Whiteboard Docs My Notes Clips

Digital Experience Opportunity

These six products together tell a story about the async + sync work hub — but they're presented as individual features rather than a unified system. The "work persistence" story that Forrester specifically credited as a strength has no equivalent narrative on the website.

Cluster 02

Customer Experience

Contact Center Virtual Agent Revenue Accelerator

Digital Experience Opportunity

This cluster is Zoom's fastest-growing revenue area — high double-digit Contact Center ARR growth, 7 of the top 10 Q4 deals displacing competitors, Virtual Agent in a near-7-figure retail deal. None of that urgency and momentum comes through on the CX section of zoom.com. The Gartner CCaaS first-ever inclusion should be impossible to miss for any CX-focused visitor.

Cluster 03

Communications

Phone Rooms Events Webinars

Digital Experience Opportunity

Zoom Phone displaced Cisco at a Fortune 10 company (140,000 seats) and added nearly 50,000 seats at a single global bank in Q4. These are some of the most compelling proof points in the entire company's story — and they're living in a press release, not front and center in the phone product experience on zoom.com.

Cluster 04

AI Platform

AI Companion Custom AI Add-on AI SDK My Notes

Digital Experience Opportunity

AI Companion monthly active users tripled year-over-year, every top 10 Contact Center deal included paid AI, and the Custom AI add-on enables agentic workflows across third-party enterprise systems. The AI story on zoom.com is told in marketing language. It should be told in before/after workflows, interactive demos, and side-by-side comparisons with Microsoft Copilot — where Zoom's "AI included" pricing model is a decisive advantage.

The pattern: Each cluster has a distinct competitive story, distinct buyer, and distinct set of proof points. When they're presented as a flat grid of icons, those stories collapse into noise. Organizing the digital experience around clusters — with narrative, proof, and conversion paths tailored to each — is the highest-leverage structural change available without touching the product itself.

What all three frameworks reveal is the same insight: zoom.com is well-built for the audience it already had — people who know what Zoom is and want to download the app or sign up for a plan. The enterprise buyer evaluating whether to displace Cisco, the CX leader comparing Zoom to Genesys, the IT leader making the internal business case for Zoom Workplace — those journeys are harder than they need to be.

That's not a criticism of the product or the team. It's a reflection of how fast the portfolio has grown. A digital experience that catches up to the platform's actual depth would close the gap between what Zoom has proven in enterprise deals and what any buyer can understand in the first three minutes on zoom.com. That's the work.

Perspectives on AI

Every member of a digital experience team should be using AI to prototype faster, test more hypotheses, and discover things that would have taken a quarter to find before.

In all of these ways, I see AI as a supercharger for teams, not a replacement. The teams that build the most leverage will be the ones who define where human judgment is irreplaceable — brand, strategy, executive relationships — and let agents own the execution layer underneath it.

This site was built with the assistance of AI tools as a demonstration of that approach. I used Perplexity for public research on Zoom's earnings, analyst reports, and competitive landscape; Claude Code to build the page; and did the writing by hand because synthesizing multiple strategic sources requires judgment that AI drafts haven't been worth editing. The speed-to-quality tradeoff matters, and knowing which tasks to hand off and which to own is the skill.

Web Operating Model

Strategy for humans.
Experience for agents.

The biggest shift in how I'd structure this team isn't headcount — it's architecture. A modern digital experience team runs experiments continuously, serves personalized content at scale, and monitors performance across every touchpoint. Humans set direction and own the decisions that move the business. Agents own the execution layer.

01 AI Agent

Experimentation Engine

Runs continuous A/B tests across value propositions, page layouts, CTAs, and audience flows. Reports wins and losses weekly. The human sets the hypotheses and prioritizes the queue; the agent runs the cycle and surfaces what's working — without waiting for a sprint to start.

02 AI Agent

Personalization Layer

Serves role- and firmographic-based content variations to visitors using company, industry, and behavioral signals. The IT lead and the CX buyer get different entry points, proof points, and next steps — without the team manually managing every segment. Human defines the logic; agent executes delivery at scale.

03 AI Agent

SEO & GEO Monitoring

Tracks keyword rankings and AI search citations daily. Flags when Zoom loses a high-intent position or fails to appear in an AI-generated answer for a topic like "UCaaS comparison" or "Cisco phone replacement." Generates content briefs automatically for every cluster that needs to exist — in traditional and AI search alike.

04 AI Agent

Journey Architecture

Maps conversion paths end-to-end across zoom.com, support.zoom.com, and campaign landing pages. Identifies where each audience type drops off, surfaces the highest-impact fixes for the human team to prioritize, and tracks improvement over time without manual reporting.

05 AI Agent

Analytics & Insight

Synthesizes behavioral data across all Zoom digital properties into a unified view — traffic, engagement, conversion, and attribution. Anyone on the team can query it for a report without waiting for an analyst sprint. The team learns faster; decisions get made on real data instead of gut feel and last quarter's deck.

06 AI Agent

Content Production

Takes a brief and produces first drafts: product page rewrites, audience-specific landing pages, case study frameworks, test variant copy. A human edits and approves before anything publishes. Collapses the brief-to-publish cycle from weeks to days — which matters when Zoom is making fast strategic moves like the agentic AI announcement or the Pindrop integration.

Humans Own

  • — Brand judgment, platform narrative, and executive relationships
  • — Roadmap prioritization and budget decisions
  • — Cross-functional alignment with product, sales, and engineering
  • — High-stakes positioning and final approval on anything that ships

Agents Own

  • — Experiment execution and continuous optimization loops
  • — Content drafts, personalization delivery, and SEO/GEO monitoring
  • — Performance monitoring and anomaly detection across all properties
  • — Reporting and ad hoc data queries for any team member, any time

The goal isn't more content or faster execution. It's a smarter experience that turns Zoom's platform strength into unmistakable competitive clarity for every buyer who lands on zoom.com.

Technical Baseline

Publicly available data on zoom.com surfaces a few signals worth paying attention to.

31K

Monthly visits arriving from AI platforms (ChatGPT, Perplexity, etc.) out of ~25M total — roughly 0.1% of all traffic. A significant GEO investment gap for a brand competing on AI.

47%

Bounce rate on zoom.com per SimilarWeb — nearly half of visitors leave without exploring a second page on a site selling a 12-product platform.

2.17

Average pages per visit across zoom.com. For a company with enterprise, CX, phone, AI, and developer audiences, visitors aren't finding adjacent content or paths.

0

Audience-specific journey paths detectable on the homepage for IT, CX, HR, or sales visitors. All six buyer types receive the same default experience.

Sources: SimilarWeb, SEMrush — March 2026 snapshots. AI platform traffic figure from SEMrush AI platform traffic report. Audience path observation from direct site review.

Zoom's biggest digital experience opportunity isn't a redesign — it's closing the gap between the platform's proven strengths and what any visitor can understand in the first three minutes on the site.

Quick Hits

From reviewing zoom.com today, there are a number of changes that could sharpen the platform story before any structural overhaul.

01

The homepage still frames Zoom as a meetings product

The hero reads "Find out what's possible when work connects" — aspirational but abstract, and the immediate follow-up is a product carousel that leads with Meetings. An IT buyer evaluating Cisco alternatives or a CX leader reading about Gartner's first Zoom CCaaS inclusion would have to dig past the meetings messaging to find what they came for.

02

Twelve products, no hierarchy

The homepage product grid surfaces Meetings, Contact Center, AI Companion, Phone, Whiteboard, Docs, Rooms, Virtual Agent, Clips, Revenue Accelerator, and more — as a flat set of equally weighted tiles. No prioritization by audience, growth area, or competitive urgency. Enterprise buyers can't immediately tell which products are for them.

03

Analyst validation is scattered, not featured

Zoom received the highest possible Forrester scores in 16 criteria, was recognized as a Customers' Choice in Gartner Peer Insights, and earned its first-ever CCaaS Magic Quadrant inclusion — but these live in blog posts and a small trust strip, not as central elements of the page. The "only Leader with above-average customer feedback" Forrester designation is buried.

04

AI Companion is described, not demonstrated

AI Companion 3.0 gets a prominent section, but it's written in marketing language — "context capture, summaries, automations." There are no interactive demos, before/after workflow comparisons, or product-led explorations showing how the AI Panel behaves differently in Docs vs. Meetings vs. Phone. The magic is hidden from the buyers who most need to see it.

05

Three digital destinations, no cohesive journey

zoom.com, support.zoom.com, and learning.zoom.us have different design systems, different navigation patterns, and different content tones. A buyer who clicks from a marketing page into the Learning Center or Support feels like they've left the site. Unifying these into a cohesive journey architecture would meaningfully raise the depth and quality of every visit.

06

The CCaaS entry point is unclear for evaluators

Zoom's Contact Center is growing at high double-digit ARR and displacing Genesys and Talkdesk in competitive deals. But a buyer who arrives via a Gartner CCaaS report looking to evaluate Zoom has to navigate the Products menu to find the Contact Center page, where the first-ever Gartner Magic Quadrant milestone isn't immediately and confidently visible.

07

Conversion paths aren't differentiated by buyer type

"Find your plan" and "Get started today" are clear CTAs — but they lead to the same pricing page regardless of whether the visitor is an SMB looking for Meetings or a Fortune 500 evaluating Contact Center. Progressive-profiling flows and segmented conversion paths would increase qualified pipeline without adding friction for self-serve buyers.

08

GEO / AI search investment appears low

Zoom.com receives approximately 31,000 visits per month from AI platforms — roughly 0.1% of total traffic. For a brand competing on the basis of being an AI-first platform, and for buyers who are increasingly using ChatGPT and Perplexity to research enterprise software, that number suggests an underinvested GEO strategy that would meaningfully reward attention in 2026.

Screenshot placeholder — zoom.com homepage

Screenshot placeholder — zoom.com product nav

A Test from My Time at NetApp

Asking Questions

One small but important test I ran at NetApp was to simply ask customers what they were looking for on the site. It started as a single-page experiment but grew into whole-site profiling efforts. The assumption: B2B buyers don't want to waste time, and will tell you what they need if you make it easy. So we just asked.

Zoom could benefit from a similar approach — profiling the visitor early in the journey and routing them accordingly. What industry are you in? Are you evaluating contact center, workplace collaboration, or phone? What team are you on? The answers don't just improve personalization; they speed up every downstream touchpoint including sales, and make the site feel like it was built for the person using it.

Thank you for the opportunity

This is an introductory look at how Zoom's digital experience could be rebuilt to lead the company's AI-first platform narrative.

Let's talk → Download Resume →

My Time at NetApp

Led a full transformation of NetApp's global demand engine — a 20,000-page site across 10 languages. In the first year after relaunch, organic traffic doubled, SQLs increased by 11%, and Forbes ranked the site #1 in B2B digital experience among Fortune 500 companies.

Founded & Acquired

Founded Emerson Stone, a B2B design and strategy agency (clients: IBM, 3M, Wells Fargo, Twitter) — acquired by Sawaya Consulting. Co-founded Mocavo, a genealogical search engine that grew to the third largest in the industry before acquisition by FindMyPast.

How I Work

I start by learning from the sales and marketing teams — to understand what's working, where investment is needed, and how to prioritize. I'm drawn to moments where the product is ahead of the story being told about it online, and where the digital experience has real leverage over growth.
